title: Part 2: Ecoregion-Based Range Inference
text: vignette focuses core ecological idea behind gbif.range: species ranges inferred occurrences, occurrences constrained ecoregions rather expanded across landscape purely geometric hull. two longer examples cover broad terrestrial workflow using Panthera tigris finer regional workflow using Arctostaphylos alpinus European Alps custom ecoregions derived environmental rasters. relevant functions : get_range() actual range inference, read_ecoreg() ecoreg_list packaged large-scale ecoregions, make_ecoreg() custom ecoregions derived set spatial raster layers, cv_range() evaluate_range() evaluating resulting maps.

title: Part 2: Ecoregion-Based Range Inference
text: get_range() designed one focal species time. algorithm originates Hagen et al. (2019) can summarized four linked operations: remove isolated spatial outliers occurrence table, identify occupied ecoregions cluster occurrences within , build convex hulls buffers within-ecoregion cluster, intersect polygons occupied ecoregions enforce ecological limits. means final output reflects geometry occurrences eco-geographic structure study area.

title: Part 2: Ecoregion-Based Range Inference
text: example deliberately small fully offline. Two synthetic rasters used create custom ecoregion layer, short occurrence table passed get_range().  Three practical points worth noting . First, get_range() accepts set occurrence coordinates input via occ_coord — just getGBIF object returned get_gbif(). data.frame decimalLongitude, decimalLatitude columns valid. means museum records, citizen science exports, expert-curated datasets, coordinates source can used directly, long follow column structure. Second, custom ecoregions created make_ecoreg() can passed directly get_range(). useful regional studies global ecoregions coarse. Third, get_range() returns getRange object. spatial output stored range_obj$rangeOutput, original inputs kept range_obj$init.args.
code: set.seed(1)  # Create two simple environmental surfaces on a small study area. r1 <- terra::rast(ncols = 20, nrows = 20, xmin = 0, xmax = 10, ymin = 0, ymax = 10) terra::values(r1) <- rep(seq(0, 1, length.out = 20), each = 20) r2 <- terra::rast(r1) terra::values(r2) <- rep(seq(0, 1, length.out = 20), times = 20) env <- c(r1, r2)  # Derive four custom ecoregions from the environmental layers. eco <- make_ecoreg(env = env, nclass = 4) #> CLARA algorithm processing... #> Generating polygons...  # Define one species as an occurrence table in the format expected by get_range(). occ <- data.frame(   input_search = "example_species",   decimalLongitude = c(0.5, 1.2, 2.4, 3.6, 2.8, 6.0, 7.2, 7.4, 7.6, 8.8),   decimalLatitude = c(1.0, 0.1, 2.3, 2.5, 2.7, 5.0, 7.1, 7.3, 6.5, 7.7) )  # Build the range from the occurrences and the ecoregion layer. range_obj <- get_range(   occ_coord = occ,   ecoreg = eco,   ecoreg_name = "EcoRegion",   verbose = FALSE )  terra::plot(   range_obj$rangeOutput,   col = "#3c8d5a",   main = "A minimal ecoregion-constrained range" ) graphics::points(occ$decimalLongitude, occ$decimalLatitude, pch = 4)

title: Part 2: Ecoregion-Based Range Inference
text: Large-scale analyses usually rely one packaged ecoregion layers: terrestrial (eco_terra; Olson et al. 2001; Nature Conservancy 2009), marine (eco_marine, eco_hd_marine; Spalding et al. 2007, 2012; Nature Conservancy 2012), freshwater (eco_fresh; Abell et al. 2008). public pattern terrestrial analysis : Beyond packaged layers, get_range() accepts polygon object ecoreg argument — SpatVector, sf, spatial polygon class — long contains character column whose name passed ecoreg_name. meanshabitat maps, expert-defined biogeographic units, bioregions derived species composition data (Denelle et al. 2025) can serve constraining layer without conversion step. contrast, regional analyses often benefit custom ecoregions built raster data make_ecoreg() (Chauvier et al. 2021). Although climate layers common input, spatially structured raster variable can used — species richness surfaces, biodiversity indices, habitat suitability maps, combination ecological drivers thought define meaningful spatial units (Denelle et al. 2025). package ships raster examples inst/extdata, including rst.tif, can used starting point: key trade-ecological detail versus robustness sampling density. Coarser ecoregions produce broader, smoother ranges. Finer ecoregions can better capture regional structure, also make results sensitive local sampling gaps.
code: vapply(ecoreg_list, `[[`, character(1), "filename") #> [1] "eco_terra"     "eco_fresh"     "eco_marine"    "eco_hd_marine" eco_terra <- read_ecoreg("eco_terra", save_dir = tempdir()) obs_tiger <- get_gbif("Panthera tigris")  range_tiger <- get_range(   occ_coord = obs_tiger,   ecoreg = eco_terra,   ecoreg_name = "ECO_NAME" ) rst <- terra::rast(ext_file("rst.tif")) my_eco <- make_ecoreg(env = rst, nclass = 200) obs_arcto <- get_gbif("Arctostaphylos alpinus",my.eco)  range_regional <- get_range(   occ_coord = obs_regional,   ecoreg = my_eco,   ecoreg_name = "EcoRegion",   res = 0.05 )

what: A broad terrestrial workflow: Panthera tigris
title: Part 2: Ecoregion-Based Range Inference
text: Tiger occurrences good example broad terrestrial use case gbif.range. workflow straightforward: retrieve occurrences coarse precision filter, load terrestrial ecoregions world, infer range inside ecoregional boundaries.  typical continental global pattern. ecoregion layer externally defined ecologically interpretable, occurrence filter intentionally conservative. practice, kind workflow gbif.range useful transparent alternative unconstrained geometric envelopes.
code: # Step 1: download global tiger occurrences with a 100 km precision filter. obs_tiger <- get_gbif(   sp_name = "Panthera tigris",   grain = 100 )  # Step 2: load the packaged terrestrial ecoregion layer. eco_terra <- read_ecoreg("eco_terra", save_dir = tempdir())  # Step 3: infer the range at the default 0.1 degree output resolution. range_tiger <- get_range(   occ_coord = obs_tiger,   ecoreg = eco_terra,   ecoreg_name = "ECO_NAME",   degrees_outlier = 5,   clust_pts_outlier = 4,   res = 0.1 )  terra::plot(range_tiger$rangeOutput, col = "#238b45") graphics::points(obs_tiger[, c("decimalLongitude", "decimalLatitude")], pch = 20)

what: A finer regional workflow: Arctostaphylos alpinus in the Alps
title: Part 2: Ecoregion-Based Range Inference
text: second workflow deliberately different. issue worldwide coverage, opposite: global terrestrial ecoregions coarse mountain system strong local climatic structure. custom ecoregion layer therefore built packaged example CHELSA bioclimatic rasters (Karger et al. 2017).  regional example shows make_ecoreg() part main package workflow rather auxiliary convenience. small spatial extents, ecological realism range map often depends choice ecoregion layer minor changes buffer parameters.
code: # Step 1: define the Alps study region and retrieve occurrences. alps_extent <- terra::vect(ext_file("shp_lonlat.shp"))  obs_arcto <- get_gbif(   sp_name = "Arctostaphylos alpinus",   geo = alps_extent,   grain = 1 )  # Step 2: create finer ecoregions from the packaged environmental rasters. rst <- terra::rast(ext_file("rst.tif")) eco_alps <- make_ecoreg(env = rst, nclass = 200)  # Step 3: infer the range at finer output resolution. range_arcto <- get_range(   occ_coord = obs_arcto,   ecoreg = eco_alps,   ecoreg_name = "EcoRegion",   res = 0.05 )  terra::plot(range_arcto$rangeOutput, col = "#3c8d5a") graphics::points(obs_arcto[, c("decimalLongitude", "decimalLatitude")], pch = 20)

code: cv_res <- cv_range(   range_object = range_obj,   cv = "block-cv",   nfolds = 3,   nblocks = 2,   backpoints = 500 ) root_dir <- system.file("extdata", package = "gbif.range")  res_eval <- evaluate_range(   root_dir = root_dir,   valData_dir = "SDM",   ecoRM_dir = "EcoRM",   print_map = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )  head(res_eval$df_eval) cv_range(   range_object = range_tiger,   cv = "block-cv",   nfolds = 5,   nblocks = 2,   backpoints = 1e4 )

what: Local example: custom ecoregions with make_ecoreg()
title: Part 0: Getting Started
text: regional analyses packaged ecoregions may coarse. make_ecoreg() builds custom ecoregion layer k-means clustering one spatial raster layers (Chauvier et al. 2021, Global Ecology Biogeography). spatially structured raster variable can used input — just climate layers. example first illustrates make_ecoreg() output looks like 10 classes European Alps, using two CHELSA bioclimatic layers (Karger et al. 2017) — mean annual temperature (bio1) annual precipitation (bio12) 5 × 5 km resolution:  real regional analysis, classes appropriate. full workflow 200 classes Arctostaphylos alpinus:  Two design choices matter . First, grain = 1 keeps records coordinate uncertainty ≤ 1 km; larger scales default 100 km grain appropriate, small alpine extent retain many imprecise records. Second, res argument sets output raster resolution, can fine input environmental layers allow.
code: bio <- terra::rast(paste0(system.file(package = "gbif.range"), "/extdata/rst.tif")) eco.eg <- make_ecoreg(env = bio, nclass = 10) terra::plot(eco.eg, col = grDevices::rainbow(10)) # Two CHELSA bioclimatic layers for the European Alps at 5 x 5 km resolution bio <- terra::rast(paste0(system.file(package = "gbif.range"), "/extdata/rst.tif"))  # 200 ecoregion classes my.eco <- make_ecoreg(env = bio, nclass = 200)  # Download Arctostaphylos alpinus within the Alps bounding box shp.lonlat <- terra::vect(   paste0(system.file(package = "gbif.range"), "/extdata/shp_lonlat.shp") ) obs.arcto <- get_gbif(   sp_name = "Arctostaphylos alpinus",   geo     = shp.lonlat,   grain   = 1            # 1 km precision — appropriate for a local extent )  # Build the range (always use 'EcoRegion' as ecoreg_name for make_ecoreg() output) range.arcto <- get_range(   occ_coord         = obs.arcto,   ecoreg            = my.eco,   ecoreg_name       = "EcoRegion",   degrees_outlier   = 5,   clust_pts_outlier = 4,   res               = 0.05        # 5 x 5 km output resolution )  # Plot alps.shp <- terra::crop(countries, terra::ext(bio)) r.arcto  <- terra::mask(range.arcto$rangeOutput, alps.shp) terra::plot(alps.shp, col = "#bcbddc") terra::plot(r.arcto, add = TRUE, col = "darkgreen", axes = FALSE, legend = FALSE) graphics::points(obs.arcto[, c("decimalLongitude", "decimalLatitude")],        pch = 20, col = "#99340470", cex = 1)

title: Part 3: Large Downloaded GBIF Tables
text: vignette documents disk-based workflow large downloaded GBIF tables. intended situations occurrences already exported GBIF full table large inconvenient keep memory. workflow built around three functions: split_gbif_by_species() split large GBIF table one file per species GBIF key, species_csvs_to_ranges() read files sequentially build one range per species, read_range_rds() read saved range outputs back disk. workflow meant point direct credential-free GBIF retrieval via rgbif (Chamberlain et al. 2022) longer practical option. many single-species moderate-volume tasks, get_gbif() enough. large downloaded exports, working files disk usually better choice.

what: Step 3: read and inspect the saved ranges
title: Part 3: Large Downloaded GBIF Tables
text: range outputs saved .rds, read_range_rds() restores simple list init.args rangeOutput. example overlays every saved range batch summary. useful diagnostic pattern checking whether batch run produced geometries expected extent shape.
code: # Read the first saved range to inspect its structure. first_range <- read_range_rds(range_summary$range_file[1]) names(first_range) #> [1] "init.args"   "rangeOutput" class(first_range$rangeOutput) #> [1] "SpatVector" #> attr(,"package") #> [1] "terra" range_colors <- c(   grDevices::rgb(0.10, 0.40, 0.75, 0.30),   grDevices::rgb(0.85, 0.35, 0.10, 0.30),   grDevices::rgb(0.20, 0.65, 0.30, 0.30),   grDevices::rgb(0.70, 0.65, 0.10, 0.30) )  combined_ext <- terra::ext(first_range$rangeOutput)  if (nrow(range_summary) > 1) {   for (i in 2:nrow(range_summary)) {     range_i <- read_range_rds(range_summary$range_file[i])     combined_ext <- terra::ext(       min(terra::xmin(combined_ext), terra::xmin(range_i$rangeOutput)),       max(terra::xmax(combined_ext), terra::xmax(range_i$rangeOutput)),       min(terra::ymin(combined_ext), terra::ymin(range_i$rangeOutput)),       max(terra::ymax(combined_ext), terra::ymax(range_i$rangeOutput))     )   } }  terra::plot(combined_ext, col = NA, legend = FALSE, main = "Batch-generated ranges") terra::plot(first_range$rangeOutput, add = TRUE, col = range_colors[1])  if (nrow(range_summary) > 1) {   for (i in 2:nrow(range_summary)) {     range_i <- read_range_rds(range_summary$range_file[i])     terra::plot(range_i$rangeOutput, add = TRUE, col = range_colors[i])   } }

title: Part 3: Large Downloaded GBIF Tables
text: disk-based workflow useful three situations. First, direct -memory call get_gbif() appropriate project already relies downloaded GBIF exports. Second, large multi-species table needs checked, cleaned, archived species level range inference. Third, species-specific occurrence files need processed repeatedly different get_range() settings, ecoregions, evaluation schemes. third point matters scientifically, just computationally. makes parameter sensitivity analyses much cleaner, can rerun archived species files alternative ecoregion layers buffering choices without changing underlying occurrence input.

what: Species Range Mapping from GBIF Using Ecoregion Constraints
title: Species Range Mapping from GBIF Using Ecoregion Constraints
text: Species ranges can estimated expert maps (example IUCN EUFORGEN) modelling approaches. Expert data, however, remain unavailable many species, whereas modelling workflows often require substantial technical expertise large numbers occurrence records. Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) — largest public repository georeferenced species observations worldwide — offer practical alternative, yet retrieving scale R remains cumbersome translating raw occurrences ecologically meaningful range maps straightforward, records often contain erroneous coordinates must identified removed spatial analyses. gbif.range provides complete workflow retrieve, clean, analyze GBIF occurrence records generate ecologically informed species range maps. Built around GBIF backbone taxonomy, package handles synonym-aware downloads, dynamic tiling large datasets, 13 configurable post-processing filters via CoordinateCleaner. Range maps constrained bundled custom ecoregion layers (terrestrial, marine, freshwater), can validated independent data using built-evaluation cross-validation functions. dedicated disk-based pipeline allows processing large multi-species GBIF exports without loading memory. Additional utilities cover GBIF taxonomy inspection, occurrence thinning, geographic tiling, GBIF-derived DOI generation. full documentation, workflows, examples, visit package website.

title: Evaluate a Range Map by Cross-Validation — cv_range
text: range_object getRange object. Typically returned get_range(). cv Character. String specifying cross-validation strategy: "random-cv" "block-cv". nfolds Numeric. Number folds. nblocks Numeric. Multiplier used cv = "block-cv" define total number spatial blocks nfolds * nblocks. backpoints Numeric. Number regularly spaced background points used pseudo-absences. Default 10000. verbose Logical. fold progress messages printed?

title: Evaluate a Range Map by Cross-Validation — cv_range
text: data frame one row per fold plus Mean row, columns TP, FA, TA, FP, Precision, Sensitivity, Specificity, TSS.

title: Evaluate a Range Map by Cross-Validation — cv_range
text: function rebuilds range map nfolds times. iteration, one fold reserved evaluation remaining folds used training. Two strategies available: random cross-validation spatial block cross-validation. latter reduces influence spatial autocorrelation grouping nearby observations splitting across folds. true absences generally unavailable, evaluation uses regular grid background points pseudo-absences reports precision, sensitivity, specificity, TSS.

what: Evaluate Range Maps Against Independent Validation Data — evaluate_range
title: Evaluate Range Maps Against Independent Validation Data — evaluate_range
text: Compare range maps produced get_range() external validation data, species distribution models (SDMs) expert-derived range maps, summarize precision, sensitivity, specificity, TSS.

title: Evaluate Range Maps Against Independent Validation Data — evaluate_range
code: evaluate_range(   root_dir = NULL,   valData_dir = NULL,   ecoRM_dir = NULL,   valData_type = NULL,   verbose = TRUE,   print_map = TRUE,   mask = NULL,   res_fact = NULL )

title: Evaluate Range Maps Against Independent Validation Data — evaluate_range
text: root_dir Character. String giving root directory contains generated range maps validation data. valData_dir Character. String giving directory, relative root_dir, containing validation data. ecoRM_dir Character. String giving directory, relative root_dir, containing range maps generated get_range(). valData_type Character. String indicating expected validation-data format: "SHP" "TIFF". verbose Logical. progress information printed comparison running? print_map Logical. TRUE, write PDF overlay map evaluated species. mask Optional SpatRaster. Used study-area mask common comparison domain. res_fact Optional integer. Aggregation factor used coarsen native resolution comparison.

title: Evaluate Range Maps Against Independent Validation Data — evaluate_range
text: list two elements: df_eval, data frame containing per-species evaluation statistics, overlay_list, list raster overlays used plotting inspection.

title: Evaluate Range Maps Against Independent Validation Data — evaluate_range
text: TIFF validation files must file names matching species names range maps. Shapefile-based validation data must include column named sci_name matching species names. function can optionally mask focal study region aggregate maps coarser resolutions calculating evaluation metrics, useful comparing products different native resolutions.

title: Evaluate Range Maps Against Independent Validation Data — evaluate_range

what: gbif.range: Tools for GBIF Retrieval and Ecoregion-Based Species Range Mapping — gbif.range-package
title: gbif.range: Tools for GBIF Retrieval and Ecoregion-Based Species Range Mapping — gbif.range-package
text: gbif.range provides workflow retrieve occurrence records GBIF, clean filter spatial analyses, assemble download ecoregion layers, generate ecologically informed species range maps, evaluate resulting maps independent data.

title: gbif.range: Tools for GBIF Retrieval and Ecoregion-Based Species Range Mapping — gbif.range-package
text: package designed around typical workflow: (1) inspect download GBIF records get_gbif_count() get_gbif(), (2) retrieve taxonomic information get_status(), (3) load packaged ecoregions read_ecoreg() create custom ones make_ecoreg(), (4) build range maps get_range(), (5) evaluate maps evaluate_range() cv_range(). Additional helpers include get_doi() creating GBIF-derived dataset DOIs, obs_filter() grid-based thinning, make_tiles() splitting study extents GBIF-ready polygons, disk-based workflow built around split_gbif_by_species(), species_csvs_to_ranges(), read_range_rds() large downloaded GBIF tables.

code: # ------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 1. Minimal in-memory workflow with custom ecoregions # -------------------------------------------------------------------------  # Create two simple environmental layers on a small synthetic study area. r1 <- terra::rast(ncols = 20, nrows = 20, xmin = 0, xmax = 10,   ymin = 0, ymax = 10) terra::values(r1) <- rep(seq(0, 1, length.out = 20), each = 20) r2 <- terra::rast(r1) terra::values(r2) <- rep(seq(0, 1, length.out = 20), times = 20) env <- c(r1, r2)  # Derive custom ecoregions and define a small occurrence table in memory. eco <- make_ecoreg(env = env, nclass = 4) #> CLARA algorithm processing...  #> Generating polygons...  occ <- data.frame(   decimalLongitude = c(0.5, 1.2, 2.4, 3.6, 2.8, 6.0, 7.2, 7.4, 7.6, 8.8),   decimalLatitude = c(1.0, 0.1, 2.3, 2.5, 2.7, 5.0, 7.1, 7.3, 6.5, 7.7) )  # Build the range directly from the occurrence table and ecoregions. range_obj <- get_range(   occ_coord = occ,   ecoreg = eco,   verbose = FALSE )  # Plot the predicted range and overlay the occurrence points. terra::plot(range_obj$rangeOutput, col = 3, main = "Range Map") graphics::points(occ, pch = 4)   # \donttest{ # ------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 2. Typical online workflow with GBIF data # -------------------------------------------------------------------------  # Download GBIF occurrences for one species. obs <- get_gbif("Panthera tigris", grain = 25) #> |--------------------------------------------| #> | Total number (all records)    :       8007 | #> | Kept records                  :       5457 | #> |--------------------------------------------| #> | Kept records according to parameters: #> | spatial_issue = FALSE, has_xy = TRUE #>  #> ...GBIF records of Panthera tigris: download starting... #>  ------------- #1 (100%..)                #>  #> ...Records (XY) filtering summary: #> ----------------------------------------------- #>                     step removed remaining #>          Grain filtering    4082      1375 #>       Duplicated records     793       582 #>          Absence records       0       582 #>          Basis selection      72       510 #>  Establishment selection       0       510 #>               Time frame       0       510 #>        Identical records       0       510 #>         Raster centroids       0       510 #>  #> Initial records         : 5457 #> Total removed           : 4947 #> Final records (XY)      : 510 #> ----------------------------------------------- #> Final records (no XY)   : 0  # Inspect the GBIF backbone interpretation used by the package. status <- get_status("Panthera tigris") status #>           canonicalName    rank gbif_key                   scientificName #> 5219416 Panthera tigris SPECIES  5219416 Panthera tigris (Linnaeus, 1758) #> 4969819    Felis tigris SPECIES  4969819      Felis tigris Linnaeus, 1758 #>         gbif_status    Genus  Family     Order    Class   Phylum IUCN_status #> 5219416    ACCEPTED Panthera Felidae Carnivora Mammalia Chordata  ENDANGERED #> 4969819     SYNONYM Panthera Felidae Carnivora Mammalia Chordata  ENDANGERED #>         sp_nameMatch #> 5219416        INPUT #> 4969819        EXACT  # Load a packaged terrestrial ecoregion layer and build the range. eco_terra <- read_ecoreg("eco_terra") tiger_range <- get_range(   occ_coord = obs,   ecoreg = eco_terra,   ecoreg_name = "ECO_NAME" ) #> ## Start of computation for species:  Panthera tigris  ###  #> 27 outlier's from 508 | proportion from total points: 5% #> ecoregion 1  of  27 :  Brahmaputra Valley Semi-Evergreen Forests  #> ecoregion 2  of  27 :  Central Deccan Plateau Dry Deciduous Forests  #> ecoregion 3  of  27 :  Chao Phraya Freshwater Swamp Forests  #> ecoregion 4  of  27 :  Eastern Highlands Moist Deciduous Forests  #> ecoregion 5  of  27 :  Eastern Himalayan Alpine Shrub And Meadows  #> ecoregion 6  of  27 :  Eastern Himalayan Broadleaf Forests  #> ecoregion 7  of  27 :  Eastern Java-Bali Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 8  of  27 :  Himalayan Subtropical Broadleaf Forests  #> ecoregion 9  of  27 :  Himalayan Subtropical Pine Forests  #> ecoregion 10  of  27 :  Kayah-Karen Montane Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 11  of  27 :  Khathiar-Gir Dry Deciduous Forests  #> ecoregion 12  of  27 :  Luang Prabang Montane Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 13  of  27 :  Meghalaya Subtropical Forests  #> ecoregion 14  of  27 :  Mizoram-Manipur-Kachin Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 15  of  27 :  Narmada Valley Dry Deciduous Forests  #> ecoregion 16  of  27 :  Orissa Semi-Evergreen Forests  #> ecoregion 17  of  27 :  Peninsular Malaysian Montane Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 18  of  27 :  Peninsular Malaysian Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 19  of  27 :  South Deccan Plateau Dry Deciduous Forests  #> ecoregion 20  of  27 :  South Western Ghats Moist Deciduous Forests  #> ecoregion 21  of  27 :  South Western Ghats Montane Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 22  of  27 :  Sumatran Lowland Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 23  of  27 :  Sumatran Montane Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 24  of  27 :  Sundarbans Mangroves  #> ecoregion 25  of  27 :  Tenasserim-South Thailand Semi-Evergreen Rain Forests  #> ecoregion 26  of  27 :  Terai-Duar Savanna And Grasslands  #> ecoregion 27  of  27 :  Upper Gangetic Plains Moist Deciduous Forests  #> ## End of computation for species:  Panthera tigris  ###   # Plot the predicted terrestrial range and the GBIF occurrences. terra::plot(   tiger_range$rangeOutput,   col = 3,   main = paste("Range:", obs$scientificName[1]))  graphics::points(   obs$decimalLongitude,   obs$decimalLatitude,   pch = 4,   col = grDevices::rgb(1, 0, 1, 0.2) )   # }  # \donttest{ # ------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 3. Large downloaded GBIF table already stored on disk # -------------------------------------------------------------------------  if (requireNamespace("data.table", quietly = TRUE)) {   # Use the bundled GBIF-style example file as a stand-in for a large download.   gbif_file <- system.file("extdata", "occ_example_2sps.csv", package = "gbif.range")    # Keep each stage in its own temporary folder so outputs are easy to inspect.   split_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "gbif_pkg_split")   occ_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "gbif_pkg_occ_min")   range_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "gbif_pkg_ranges")    # Remove earlier temporary outputs so rerunning the example starts cleanly.   unlink(split_dir, recursive = TRUE)   unlink(occ_dir, recursive = TRUE)   unlink(range_dir, recursive = TRUE)    # Split the input table into one occurrence file per speciesKey without   # loading the full file into memory.   split_summary <- split_gbif_by_species(     input_file = gbif_file,     outdir = split_dir,     chunk_size = 10,     sep_in = "\t",     sep_out = "\t",     overwrite = TRUE,     verbose = FALSE   )    split_summary[, c("species_name", "n_records", "species_file")]    # Use the packaged terrestrial ecoregions for the batch range step.   # species_csvs_to_ranges() will resolve "eco_terra" with read_ecoreg().   range_summary <- species_csvs_to_ranges(     species_dir = split_dir,     ecoreg = "eco_terra",     ecoreg_name = "ECO_NAME",     outdir = range_dir,     occ_outdir = occ_dir,     occ_save_as = "tsv",     range_save_as = "rds",     sep_in = "\t",     overwrite = TRUE,     degrees_outlier = 30,     clust_pts_outlier = 2,     buff_width_point = 1,     buff_incrmt_pts_line = 0.1,     buff_width_polygon = 1,     format = "SpatVector",     verbose = FALSE   )    range_summary[, c("species_name", "n_points", "range_file")]    # Read the saved ranges back from disk and plot both species together.   range_one <- read_range_rds(range_summary$range_file[1])   range_two <- read_range_rds(range_summary$range_file[2])    combined_ext <- terra::ext(     min(terra::xmin(range_one$rangeOutput), terra::xmin(range_two$rangeOutput)),     max(terra::xmax(range_one$rangeOutput), terra::xmax(range_two$rangeOutput)),     min(terra::ymin(range_one$rangeOutput), terra::ymin(range_two$rangeOutput)),     max(terra::ymax(range_one$rangeOutput), terra::ymax(range_two$rangeOutput))   )    terra::plot(combined_ext, col = NA, legend = FALSE)   terra::plot(range_one$rangeOutput, col = grDevices::rgb(0.1, 0.6, 0.2, 0.5), add = TRUE)   terra::plot(range_two$rangeOutput, col = grDevices::rgb(0.8, 0.3, 0.1, 0.5), add = TRUE) }   # }

title: Download and Filter GBIF Occurrences for Spatial Analyses — get_gbif
text: function follows taxonomic matching logic used GBIF website. Internally, input name first resolved GBIF backbone taxonomy. matched name synonym, download uses corresponding accepted GBIF taxon key rather synonym key . Querying accepted taxon key means occurrence records linked infra-specific taxa (subspecies, varieties) accepted name also retrieved, mirroring GBIF website behaviour. acceptedTaxonKey scientificName columns output therefore reflect original GBIF record-level assignment may refer subspecies variety rather species-level input name. Use get_status(children = TRUE) beforehand inspect infra-specific keys fall queried taxon concept. output preserves original GBIF taxonomic fields record rather rewriting single accepted name. particular, scientificName stores record-level GBIF name, acceptedScientificName, acceptedTaxonKey, taxonomicStatus reflect harmonized taxon concept used query. Records linked non-taxonomic backbone entries (e.g. BOLD barcode sequences) may also appear output, since GBIF associates accepted taxon key. records typically carry basisOfRecord = "MATERIAL_SAMPLE". Note however MATERIAL_SAMPLE reliable proxy sequence-based records across taxonomic groups. Users can cross-check returned acceptedTaxonKey values get_status(children = TRUE) identify records linked non-backbone entries; see get_status() examples. requested extent contains many records, query fragmented spatial tiles individual API calls remain manageable. Tiles refined contains fewer 10,000 records, faster robust relying single large request. Post-download filtering can remove records outside study extent, duplicated coordinates, absences, unwanted bases record, records low spatial precision, suspicious coordinate conversions, raster-centroid records. grain argument controls coordinate-uncertainty filtering minimum number coordinate decimals must reported. Decimal filtering follows thresholds: 110 km > grain \(\ge\) 11 km, coordinates least 1 decimal kept 11 km > grain \(\ge\) 1100 m, coordinates least 2 decimals kept 1100 m > grain \(\ge\) 110 m, coordinates least 3 decimals kept 110 m > grain \(\ge\) 11 m, coordinates least 4 decimals kept 11 m > grain \(\ge\) 1.1 m, coordinates least 5 decimals kept

Accepted classes SpatialPolygonsDataFrame, SpatVector, sf. can downloaded layer read_ecoreg() custom layer created make_ecoreg(). ecoreg_name Character. String naming field ecoreg defines ecoregion categories. eco_terra, example, "ECO_NAME" detailed level. ecoreg comes make_ecoreg(), "EcoRegion" used automatically. degrees_outlier Numeric. Distance threshold degrees. Points whose clust_pts_outlier-th nearest neighbour lies beyond distance treated outliers. Default 5. clust_pts_outlier Numeric. k-nearest-neighbour order used outlier detection. Default 4. buff_width_point Numeric. Buffer width degrees isolated points. buff_incrmt_pts_line Numeric. Increment buffer width linear clusters. buff_width_polygon Numeric. Buffer width degrees applied convex hull polygons. dir_temp Character. String giving directory used temporary convex-hull files. Defaults tempdir(). format Character. Output format range map. One "SpatVector" (default), "sf", "SpatRaster". "SpatRaster" rasterizes range resolution set res. res Numeric. Output resolution degrees format = "SpatRaster". Default 0.1 (11.1 km equator). achievable resolution constrained spatial precision ecoreg. verbose Logical. progress messages printed?

title: Create Species Range Maps from Occurrences and Ecoregions — get_range
text: object class getRange two fields: init.args, containing arguments data used build map, rangeOutput, containing resulting SpatVector, sf, SpatRaster object.

title: Create Species Range Maps from Occurrences and Ecoregions — get_range
text: function follows four main steps. First, occurrence points filtered spatial outliers using k-nearest-neighbour distances assigned ecoregions. Second, points within occupied ecoregions grouped clusters using combination Gaussian mixture modeling k-means clustering. Third, cluster converted polygon. Single points receive circular buffers, collinear clusters receive line-based buffers, clusters receive buffered convex hulls conv_function(). Fourth, cluster polygons intersected parent ecoregions merged final range layer. Download-ready ecoregion datasets include eco_terra terrestrial species, eco_fresh freshwater species, eco_marine eco_hd_marine marine species.

what: Retrieve Taxonomy and IUCN Status from GBIF — get_status
title: Retrieve Taxonomy and IUCN Status from GBIF — get_status
text: Query GBIF backbone taxonomy species name, report matched accepted name synonyms, return associated IUCN status available.

title: Retrieve Taxonomy and IUCN Status from GBIF — get_status
code: get_status(   sp_name = NULL,   search = TRUE,   rank = NULL,   phylum = NULL,   class = NULL,   order = NULL,   family = NULL,   conf_match = 80,   level = c("accepted", "children", "all"),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Retrieve Taxonomy and IUCN Status from GBIF — get_status
text: sp_name Character. String species name. Scientific names genus-species level expected; fuzzy matching available search = FALSE. search Logical. TRUE (default), use strict GBIF backbone search keep species-, subspecies-, variety-level matches. FALSE, use permissive search optionally rely rank, phylum, class, order, family resolve ambiguous matches. rank Character. String giving preferred rank keep: "SPECIES", "SUBSPECIES", "VARIETY". NULL, rank priority inferred sp_name. phylum Optional character. Phylum used disambiguate alternative GBIF matches. Particularly useful hemihomonyms. class Optional character. Class used disambiguate alternative GBIF matches. order Optional character. Order used disambiguate alternative GBIF matches. family Optional character. Family used disambiguate alternative GBIF matches. conf_match Numeric. Confidence threshold 0 100 GBIF backbone match. Default 80. level Character. Controls much GBIF taxon concept returned. "accepted" (default) returns accepted name synonyms. "children" additionally includes infra-specific taxa (subspecies, varieties) CHILDREN rows, mirroring scope get_gbif(). "" adds alternative name string representations (orthographic variants, etc.) RELATED rows, used get_gbif() intended taxonomic inspection . verbose Logical. status messages printed console match found? Default TRUE.

title: Retrieve Taxonomy and IUCN Status from GBIF — get_status
text: data frame columns canonicalName, rank, gbif_key, scientificName, gbif_status, Genus, Family, Order, Class, Phylum, IUCN_status, sp_nameMatch. row gbif_status = "ACCEPTED" identifies accepted GBIF taxon concept used get_gbif(), sp_nameMatch marks row closely matches submitted input name. Additional rows carry gbif_status = "CHILDREN" (infra-specific taxa, level = "children" level = "") gbif_status = "RELATED" (alternative name representations, level = "").

title: Retrieve Taxonomy and IUCN Status from GBIF — get_status
text: Use get_status() get_gbif() want inspect GBIF resolves input name, name treated accepted backbone taxon, synonyms included taxon concept used occurrence retrieval. level = "accepted", returned rows correspond accepted name synonyms linked accepted GBIF taxon key. level = "children", infra-specific taxa get_gbif() also retrieves added, giving complete picture occurrence download scope. level = "", alternative name representations included taxonomic review. IUCN Red List status retrieved GBIF accepted taxon key . GBIF currently store subspecies-level IUCN assessments, IUCN_status column uniform across rows. Note subspecies independent IUCN assessments (e.g. Panthera tigris sumatrae Critically Endangered) reflected ; consult IUCN Red List directly infra-specific status.

code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("data.table", quietly = TRUE)) {   gbif_file <- system.file("extdata", "occ_example_2sps.csv", package = "gbif.range")   split_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "gbif_read_range_split")   range_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "gbif_read_range_out")    unlink(split_dir, recursive = TRUE)   unlink(range_dir, recursive = TRUE)    split_gbif_by_species(     input_file = gbif_file,     outdir = split_dir,     chunk_size = 10,     sep_in = "\t",     sep_out = "\t",     overwrite = TRUE,     verbose = FALSE   )    occ_example <- utils::read.delim(gbif_file, sep = "\t", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)   eco_terra <- read_ecoreg(     "eco_terra",     save_dir = tempdir()   )   eco_crop <- terra::crop(     eco_terra,     terra::ext(       min(occ_example$decimalLongitude) - 5,       max(occ_example$decimalLongitude) + 5,       min(occ_example$decimalLatitude) - 5,       max(occ_example$decimalLatitude) + 5     )   )    range_summary <- species_csvs_to_ranges(     species_dir = split_dir,     ecoreg = eco_crop,     ecoreg_name = "ECO_NAME",     outdir = range_dir,     range_save_as = "rds",     sep_in = "\t",     overwrite = TRUE,     degrees_outlier = 30,     clust_pts_outlier = 2,     buff_width_point = 1,     buff_incrmt_pts_line = 0.1,     buff_width_polygon = 1,     format = "SpatVector",     verbose = FALSE   )    rg <- read_range_rds(range_summary$range_file[1])   terra::plot(rg$rangeOutput) }   # }

title: gbif.range 1.6.3
text: Added pkgdown website. Added Part 0 vignette “Getting Started” pre-computed figures. Updated vignettes pre-computed figures. Updated README.md. Updated parameters get_gbif() get_status().

title: gbif.range 1.6.2
text: Updated examples documentation. Updated vignettes. Updated get_status(). Corrected example issues code incompatibility.

title: gbif.range 1.6.1
text: Updated get_status(): new level parameter. Minor naming fix. Updated get_gbif(): new progress bars.

title: gbif.range 1.6.0
text: Added disk-based batch workflow large multi-species GBIF exports: split_gbif_by_species(), species_csvs_to_ranges(), read_range_rds(). Added Collate field DESCRIPTION explicit R file load ordering. Added three focused workflow vignettes. Updated README.md Vignettes section.

title: gbif.range 1.5.3
text: Improved documentation package get_status(). Updated get_gbif() new backend support (occ_download parameters)

title: gbif.range 1.5.2
text: Added area_data: bundled dataset gbif.range- vs. IUCN-derived range area estimates, added alongside new examples mirroring draft paper plots. Updated test routine. Updated get_status().

title: gbif.range 1.5.1
text: Clarified taxonomic harmonization get_gbif() documentation. Updated gbif.range Rd.

title: gbif.range 1.5.0
text: Polished documentation CI. Updated evaluation functions examples. Added get_gbif_count(): estimate record volume downloading. Changing functions name : make_ecoreg(), get_ecoreg(), check_and_get_ecoreg()

title: gbif.range 1.4.7
text: Added package-level help page. Updated get_gbif(). Used && GBIF status checks. Updated .gitignore README.md.

title: gbif.range 1.4.0
text: Added evaluate_range(): validate range maps independent distribution data (SDMs, IUCN polygons) precision, sensitivity, specificity, TSS. Added cv_range(): cross-validate get_range() output occurrence data using spatial random folds. Added make_blocks(): split observations balanced random spatially structured folds cross-validation workflows. Added area_data: bundled dataset gbif.range- vs. IUCN-derived range area estimates validation examples. Added internal helper functions (helpers.R) argument checking shared utilities across functions. Introduced formal R5 reference classes getRange getGBIF (classes.R) store function outputs original arguments. Added check_and_get_bioreg() get_bioreg() helpers ecoregion download caching.

title: gbif.range 1.1.0
text: Added make_ecoregion(): build custom ecoregion layers environmental rasters via k-means clustering. Moved dependencies Depends Imports cleaner namespace handling. Added sf cluster dependencies. Expanded get_range() additional ecoregion flexibility resolution control via res argument.

title: gbif.range 1.0.0
text: First stable release name gbif.range GitHub. Full migration raster terra (SpatRaster/SpatVector compatibility). Renamed get_taxonomy() get_status(): added IUCN Red List status retrieval infra-specific taxa (subspecies, varieties) lookup. Improved get_gbif() synonym handling tiling robustness. Added read_ecoreg() ecoreg_list bundled ecoregion management.

title: gbif.range 0.2.0
text: Renamed package wsl.gbif gbif.range. function containing ‘wsl’: wsl_doi() get_doi(), wsl_gbif() get_gbif, wsl_obs_filter() obs_filter() Added get_range(): ecoregion-constrained species range inference. Added conv_function(): internal polygon builder used get_range(). Added get_taxonomy() (later renamed get_status()): GBIF backbone taxonomy inspection including accepted names synonyms. Expanded get_gbif() dynamic moving-window tiling > 100,000 records improved synonym-aware downloads. Added ClusterR, FNN, geometry, mclust, rgeos dependencies.
